perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise provided for in this agreement, the above- mentioned obligations of this Section II shall apply in each case for a period of 3 (three) years after the point in time at which the respective confidential information was disclosed by M&R or made accessible to the Partner.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise provided for in this agreement, the above- mentioned obligations of this Section II shall apply in each case for a period of 1 (one) year after the point in time at which the respective confidential information was disclosed by M&R or made accessible to the Partner.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, especially in Austria where the contract is governed by Austrian law. Austrian law provides indefinite protection for trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and have commercial value.
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian law provides for the protection of trade secrets. Limiting the confidentiality to one year may fail to provide adequate protection for trade secrets that require a longer period to remain commercially valuable, thereby undermining the intent of the law which aims to protect valuable business information. Under UWG § 20c, trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and possess commercial value. A one-year limit could be deemed unreasonably short for certain types of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Law Explanation states that Austrian law protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and possess commercial value. The Law Explanation further notes that a one-year limit could be deemed unreasonably short for certain types of trade secrets, thus undermining the intent of the law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references UWG and Geschäftsgeheimnissen (trade secrets), providing legal grounding for the protection of trade secrets. Therefore, the modification creates a contradiction by potentially failing to provide adequate protection for trade secrets as required under Austrian law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this agreement, all the confidential information made available by M&R shall be returned to M&R on request of M&R, all copies that were made shall be destroyed or handed over to M&R and all stored information shall be deleted.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this agreement, the partner is not required to return confidential information made available by M&R, nor are they required to destroy or delete copies or stored information.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination, which could violate trade secret laws if the information qualifies as a trade secret under Austrian law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text potentially enables the misappropriation of trade secrets. Austrian law, specifically UWG § 20b, protects against the unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets. The failure to ensure the return or destruction of confidential information could facilitate such unlawful acts, contradicting the law's intent to safeguard trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination, which directly contradicts the intention to protect trade secrets as outlined in the Law Explanation. The provided snippet from the Bundesgesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb (UWG) discusses Verletzung von Geschäfts- oder Betriebsgeheimnissen (§§ 11 – 12) and Begriffsbestimmungen (§ 26b UWG), indicating legal concern regarding the protection of business and trade secrets. By removing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of unlawful use or disclosure of these secrets, which the UWG aims to prevent, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Partner shall protect the confidential information disclosed to him in connection with the project or to which he is given access in connection with the project from being disclosed to third parties, being used by third parties or being published at least with the same diligence he applies in order to protect his own confidential information of identical importance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Partner shall protect the confidential information disclosed to him in connection with the project or to which he is given access in connection with the project from being disclosed to third parties, being used by third parties or being published as he sees fit.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the standard of care required to protect confidential information, potentially violating Austrian trade secret laws if the partner's actions result in the disclosure or misuse of a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the partner to protect confidential information as 'he sees fit,' the modified text eliminates the diligence requirement, which contradicts Austrian trade secret laws. UWG § 20c requires adequate measures to protect trade secrets. This modification permits a level of protection that may fall short of what is legally required to prevent unauthorized access, use, or disclosure, thus conflicting with the statute.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a specific 'diligence' standard for protecting confidential information, aligning with trade secret protection laws. The changed text replaces this standard with 'as he sees fit,' removing any concrete obligation. The Law Explanation states that UWG § 20c requires adequate measures to protect trade secrets. The scraped law snippet from UWG includes multiple sections related to the 'Bundesgesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb' (Federal Law Against Unfair Competition), and specifically references '§ 11 UWG Verletzung von Geschäfts- oder Betriebsgeheimnissen. Mißbrauch anvertrauter Vorlagen', and '§ 26c UWG Rechtswidriger Erwerb, rechtswidrige Nutzung und rechtswidrige Offenlegung von Geschäftsgeheimnissen', indicating Austrian law protects trade secrets. The modification weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially allowing actions that fail to meet the legal requirement to prevent unauthorized access, use, or disclosure. Therefore, the change contradicts Austrian trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: If further third parties are involved in the project, the Partner shall not disclose or make available confidential information to the third party before M&R has approved of that and the third party has also committed himself to strict confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: If further third parties are involved in the project, the Partner may disclose confidential information to the third party without requiring M&R's approval or the third party's commitment to confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the partner to disclose confidential information to third parties without M&R's consent or a confidentiality agreement, increasing the risk of misappropriation and violating trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing disclosure to third parties without confidentiality agreements, the modification enables unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets, directly conflicting with UWG § 20b. The law protects against such misappropriation, and this change removes a critical safeguard, thus undermining the legal protections afforded to trade secrets under Austrian law.

perturbation.justification: The change allows the Partner to disclose confidential information without M&R's approval or a confidentiality agreement, which contradicts the intent of trade secret protections. The Law Explanation states this enables unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets, conflicting with UWG § 20b. The scraped law snippet 2 mentions UWG (Gesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb), indicating this law deals with fair competition, and specifically lists "Verletzung von Geschäfts- oder Betriebsgeheimnissen" (§§ 11 – 12), which translates to "Violation of business or trade secrets". The original text provides a safeguard against misappropriation, which the changed text removes.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall become effective upon signing and has a term of 5 (five) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall become effective upon signing and has a term of 6 (six) months.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the term of the agreement to six months may not provide sufficient protection for trade secrets that require a longer period to remain confidential and commercially valuable. Under Austrian law, trade secrets are protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and have commercial value. A six month protection period can contradict this.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Austrian law does not explicitly mandate a minimum term for confidentiality agreements, setting a term of only six months may be deemed inadequate to protect certain types of trade secrets, thereby undermining the intent of the UWG § 20c, which aims to protect valuable business information indefinitely as long as it remains a secret.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the agreement term from five years to six months. The Perturbation Explanation states this may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets under Austrian law, as trade secrets are protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and have commercial value. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a six-month term may be inadequate to protect certain types of trade secrets, undermining the intent of UWG § 20c. The scraped law snippet shows the UWG focuses on preventing unfair competition, including the violation of business secrets. The change creates a potential contradiction because the reduced term may not align with the indefinite protection that Austrian law provides for trade secrets, as reflected in the snippets reference to UWG and the explanations provided.
